children.name: Ursus
children.description: The genus of 'true bears' — large, plantigrade omnivores with heavy bodies, thick fur, short tails, and non-retractable claws. Ursus species dominate the temperate and polar landscapes of the Northern Hemisphere and are among the largest terrestrial predators on Earth. Despite being classified as carnivores, most species are strongly omnivorous, eating anything from berries and fish to roots and carrion. The Brown Bear holds the greatest geographic range of any ursid, stretching from the Iberian Peninsula to Kamchatka and from the Arctic tundra to the Himalayas.

children.name: Ursus arctos
children.description: The world's most widely distributed bear. Swedish populations (the björn) have recovered strongly since hunting protections; Finland retains Europe's largest brown bear population.

children.name: Ursus maritimus
children.description: The largest land carnivore, adapted to life on Arctic sea ice. White fur hides a black skin that absorbs sunlight; hollow guard hairs trap heat. Classified as vulnerable due to sea-ice loss.

children.name: Ursus americanus
children.description: North America's most numerous bear. Despite the name, colour varies from jet black to cinnamon, cream, and even white (Kermode or 'spirit bear' form).

children.name: Ursus thibetanus
children.description: Recognisable by a white or cream crescent on its chest. Highly arboreal compared to other large bears; forages in trees for nuts and fruit.

children.name: Ailuropoda
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the Giant Panda — one of the most recognisable animals on Earth and an icon of conservation. Despite being a member of the order Carnivora, the Giant Panda subsists almost entirely on bamboo, eating up to 12 kg per day. It has a specialised 'pseudo-thumb' — an enlarged radial sesamoid bone — that helps it grip bamboo stems. Wild population has recovered to over 1,800 individuals through intensive protection of habitat corridors in Sichuan, Shaanxi, and Gansu.

children.name: Ailuropoda melanoleuca
children.description: Downlisted from Endangered to Vulnerable in 2016 following decades of protection. The Qinling Panda (ssp. qinlingensis) has a brown-and-white rather than black-and-white coat.

children.name: Helarctos
children.description: The Sun Bear — the smallest of all bears, native to the tropical forests of Southeast Asia. It is a powerful climber with strongly curved claws and an enormously long tongue (up to 25 cm) adapted to extracting honey from bees' nests and insects from bark cavities. Largely nocturnal, it rests in trees by day, bending branches into crude sleeping platforms. Populations are declining due to deforestation and the illegal wildlife trade.

children.name: Helarctos malayanus
children.description: Named for the pale, often orange-gold crescent on its chest. Classified as Vulnerable; numbers halved in the last 30 years due to forest clearance and hunting.

children.name: Melursus
children.description: The Sloth Bear — a shaggy, dust-coloured bear of the Indian subcontinent, specialised for feeding on insects. It has a long muzzle, mobile lips, and a gap in its upper front teeth, which together form a biological vacuum cleaner: the bear blows away debris then sucks termites and ants directly into its mouth with an audible slurp audible from 100 metres. It is the only bear species that carries its young on its back.

children.name: Melursus ursinus
children.description: Listed as Vulnerable. Its spectacularly long lower lip and tongue are uniquely adapted for insectivory. Responsible for more fatal attacks on humans in India than any other large predator.

children.name: Tremarctos
children.description: The Spectacled Bear — South America's only bear and the last surviving member of the short-faced bear lineage Tremarctinae. Named for the pale facial markings that form spectacle-like rings around the eyes — though these vary so much between individuals that researchers can identify bears by their facial patterns alone. It is highly arboreal for a bear of its size, building feeding platforms in trees to access fruit and bromeliad hearts.

children.name: Tremarctos ornatus
children.description: The only bear native to South America and the largest land carnivore on the continent. Classified as Vulnerable; mainly threatened by habitat loss in the Andean cloud forests.
